findings.title: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag detected: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker who can compromise the GitHub Action owner's account or the repository where the action is hosted, or who can perform a dependency confusion attack.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker identifies that the workflow uses a mutable tag (e.g., `v1`) for an action. 2. Attacker gains control of the action's repository (e.g., via compromised credentials, social engineering, or repository takeover). 3. Attacker pushes a malicious commit to the tag, silently updating the action's code. 4. On the next workflow run, the malicious code executes with the permissions of the workflow (e.g., `GITHUB_TOKEN`).
attack_scenario.impact: The attacker can execute arbitrary code in the CI/CD pipeline, potentially exfiltrating secrets, modifying artifacts, or compromising the software supply chain.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding correctly identifies a mutable tag reference in a GitHub Actions workflow. The attack path is well-documented and has been exploited in real-world incidents (e.g., trivy-action, kics-github-action). The impact is severe as it can lead to supply-chain compromise.

findings.cwe: CWE-1357: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component
findings.tool_output: Rule: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software and Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
findings.description:
Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The finding is a duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-003 through IRON-SEMGREP-013. All point to the same line (64) with the same description. Without unique code context, it's impossible to verify each individually. Additionally, the description mentions 'requires login' which is not a code snippet, making it impossible to trace the actual data flow. Therefore, these are false positives due to lack of concrete evidence.]
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-002. Same reasoning applies.]]
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-002. Same reasoning applies.]]
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-002. Same reasoning applies.]]

attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is a duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-003 through IRON-SEMGREP-013. All point to the same line (64) with the same description. Without unique code context, it's impossible to verify each individually. Additionally, the description mentions 'requires login' which is not a code snippet, making it impossible to trace the actual data flow. Therefore, these are false positives due to lack of concrete evidence.

findings.tool_output: Rule: semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect: Data from request is passed to redirect(). This is an open redirect and could be exploited. Consider using 'url_for()' to generate links to known locations. If you must use a URL to unknown pages, consider using 'urlparse()' or similar and checking if the 'netloc' property is the same as your site's host name. See the references for more information.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect detected: Data from request is passed to redirect(). This is an open redirect and could be exploited. Consider using 'url_for()' to generate links to known locations. If you must use a URL to unknown pages, consider using 'urlparse()' or similar and checking if the 'netloc' property is the same as your site's host name. See the references for more information.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The finding indicates an open redirect via redirect(request.url). According to the rules, redirect(request.url) is always a false positive because it redirects to the same URL the user is already on, not an attacker-controlled destination.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The finding indicates an open redirect via redirect(request.url). According to the rules, redirect(request.url) is always a false positive because it redirects to the same URL the user is already on, not an attacker-controlled destination.

findings.cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect | Severity: ERROR | OWASP: [A01:2021 - Broken Access Control A01:2025 - Broken Access Control]

findings.title: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
findings.description: Semgrep rule html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity detected: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can compromise the CDN or perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the CDN delivery path.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker compromises the CDN serving the external script (e.g., cdn.example.com). 2. Attacker modifies the script to include malicious JavaScript (e.g., XSS payload). 3. User visits the application, browser loads the script without integrity check. 4. Malicious script executes in user's browser, stealing session cookies or performing actions on behalf of the user.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the application's origin, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement.
attack_scenario.explanation: Missing SRI on CDN scripts is a real supply chain risk. The finding at line 7 of base.html lacks integrity attribute, allowing an attacker who compromises the CDN to inject malicious code.

findings.cwe: CWE-353: Missing Support for Integrity Check
findings.tool_output: Rule: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
findings.description: Semgrep rule html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity detected: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can compromise the CDN or perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the CDN delivery path.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker compromises the CDN serving the external script (e.g., cdn.example.com). 2. Attacker modifies the script to include malicious JavaScript (e.g., XSS payload). 3. User visits the application, browser loads the script without integrity check. 4. Malicious script executes in user's browser, stealing session cookies or performing actions on behalf of the user.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the application's origin, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement.
attack_scenario.explanation: Missing SRI on CDN scripts is a real supply chain risk. The finding at line 55 of base.html lacks integrity attribute, allowing an attacker who compromises the CDN to inject malicious code.

findings.cwe: CWE-353: Missing Support for Integrity Check
findings.tool_output: Rule: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated attacker who can craft a malicious HTML page and trick a logged-in user into visiting it.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker creates a page with a form that submits to the application's endpoint (e.g., /upload) with hidden fields. 2. Attacker lures a logged-in user to visit the malicious page. 3. User's browser submits the form, performing an action (e.g., file upload) without the user's consent. 4. The application processes the request because no CSRF token is present.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can perform state-changing actions (e.g., upload files, change settings) on behalf of the victim user without their knowledge.
attack_scenario.explanation: The form at line 32 of index.html lacks a CSRF token, making it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. This is a real finding despite the rule name mentioning Django; the vulnerability pattern applies to any framework.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

The application processes the request because no CSRF token is present.

attack_scenario.explanation: The form at line 7 of login.html lacks a CSRF token, making it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. This is a real finding despite the rule name mentioning Django; the vulnerability pattern applies to any framework.

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The rule name mentions Django, but the code is a Flask app. However, the absence of a CSRF token in a form that performs state-changing operations (file upload) is a real CSRF vulnerability regardless of framework. The attacker can trick an authenticated user into submitting the form, causing unintended actions. The finding is real.]

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated attacker with ability to craft and send cross-site requests
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker hosts a malicious page that submits a form to the target Flask app's upload endpoint. 2. The form lacks a CSRF token, so the request is accepted. 3. The attacker's request triggers file upload or other state-changing action on behalf of an authenticated victim.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can perform state-changing actions (e.g., file upload) as the victim without their consent, potentially leading to data corruption or unauthorized file storage.
attack_scenario.explanation: The rule name mentions Django, but the code is a Flask app. However, the absence of a CSRF token in a form that performs state-changing operations (file upload) is a real CSRF vulnerability regardless of framework. The attacker can trick an authenticated user into submitting the form, causing unintended actions. The finding is real.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.app-run-param-config.avoid_app_run_with_bad_host: Running flask app with host 0.0.0.0 could expose the server publicly.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.app-run-param-config.avoid_app_run_with_bad_host detected: Running flask app with host 0.0.0.0 could expose the server publicly.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Running on 0.0.0.0 without debug=True is a common production configuration and is not inherently critical. The finding is a false positive because the severity is overstated; the actual risk is low and depends on network configuration.]

attack_scenario.actor: any attacker on the same network or internet if the server is exposed
attack_scenario.path: 1. The Flask app is run with host='0.0.0.0', binding to all network interfaces. 2. If the server is not behind a firewall or reverse proxy, external attackers can reach the application. 3. No further steps needed; the exposure is immediate.
attack_scenario.impact: The application becomes accessible to any network that can reach the server, increasing the attack surface. However, without debug=True, the risk is limited to potential network-level attacks. The impact is low unless combined with other vulnerabilities.
attack_scenario.explanation: Running on 0.0.0.0 without debug=True is a common production configuration and is not inherently critical. The finding is a false positive because the severity is overstated; the actual risk is low and depends on network configuration.

findings.cwe: CWE-668: Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.app-run-param-config.avoid_app_run_with_bad_host | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A01:2021 - Broken Access Control A01:2025 - Broken Access Control]

findings.title: SBOM generated: 17 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 17 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).

findings.title: 1/10 recent commits are not GPG signed
findings.description: Unsigned commits can be forged by attackers who gain access to git configuration. GPG-sign all commits to establish cryptographic proof of authorship.
findings.fix_suggestion: Configure git GPG signing: git config --global commit.gpgsign true

findings.title: Unpinned GitHub Action in deploy.yml
findings.description: GitHub Actions should be pinned to commit SHA, not version tags. Tags can be moved by attackers who compromise the action repository (GhostAction 2025 attack vector).
findings.fix_suggestion: Pin actions to full commit SHA: uses: actions/checkout@a81bb... instead of @v4

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
